paper_title: Chemical Analysis of Pozzolanicity and Mechanical Strength Testing

methods.method_name: Pozzolanic Reactivity Analysis (CaO Solubility Curve Method)
methods.description: This method assesses the pozzolanic character and reactivity intensity of samples containing diatomite and Portland cement by analyzing their position relative to the isothermal curve of CaO solubility at specific time points (8 and 15 days). A position below the curve indicates pozzolanic character, and relative positions indicate intensity.

steps.description: At 8 days of testing (Figure 5a), all analyzed samples are at a visibly deep position below the isothermal curve of CaO solubility, and this factor itself definitively determines the pozzolanic character of the sample [29].
steps.searchable_summary: Determine pozzolanic character by observing sample position relative to CaO solubility curve at 8 days.
inputs: Sample data points at 8 days (from Pozzolanic Reactivity Test)
outputs: Determination of pozzolanic character (yes/no) for each sample

steps.description: Although the general trend in this period is a spatial concentration of all the results, the truth is that there are small variations that are relevant and should be highlighted. First of all, the samples of diatomite in its natural state (NDT) are located in an orderly manner and descend on the left side of the graph. The most pozzolanic is composed of the ratio NDT/PC: 10%, and the least pozzolanic is NDT/PC: 40%, which leaves sample NDT/PC: 25% in an intermediate position. Immediately to the right are the samples in a calcined state (CDT/PC: 10-25-40%), which follow a pattern similar to the previous ones. It is evident that the pozzolanic properties are increased from the CDT/PC formulations: 40% to the CDT/PC: 10%. Although the positions of both groups of samples in the graph are very close to each other, it can be established that heat treatment significantly improves the pozzolanic character [39].
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze spatial distribution and relative positions of different sample formulations (NDT/PC, CDT/PC, varying ratios) on the graph at 8 days to compare pozzolanic intensity and effect of calcination.
outputs: Relative pozzolanic intensity ranking for formulations at 8 days
Assessment of calcination effect on pozzolanicity at 8 days

steps.description: At 15 days (Figure 5b), the pozzolanic reactivity of all samples continues to manifest, even though they maintain some spatial proximity. A noteworthy fact, however, is the linear pattern the samples follow, whereby the most pozzolanic (CDT/PC: 10%) is in the deepest position on the right-hand side of the graph, while the least pozzolanic (CDT/PC: 40%) is located towards the extreme left.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze spatial proximity and linear pattern of samples on the graph at 15 days to assess continued pozzolanic reactivity and compare formulations.
inputs: Sample data points at 15 days (from Pozzolanic Reactivity Test)
outputs: Assessment of continued pozzolanic reactivity at 15 days
Relative pozzolanic intensity ranking for formulations at 15 days

steps.description: According to the results, it is evident that there are two key factors that determine the intensity with which the pozzolanic reactivity manifests: the partial replacement of Portland cement by diatomite and the degree of calcination applied to said sample.
steps.searchable_summary: Identify key factors (replacement ratio, calcination) influencing pozzolanic reactivity intensity based on analysis of results at 8 and 15 days.
outputs: Identification of key influencing factors for pozzolanic reactivity

methods.description: This method involves analyzing the results from mechanical strength tests performed on specimens with different diatomite/Portland cement formulations at various curing times (7, 28, and 90 days) to evaluate their mechanical performance, assess the effect of diatomite inclusion, and confirm pozzolanic effects.

steps.description: Figure 6a,b graphically show the results obtained by the mechanical strength test (MST) at 7, 28, and 90 days.
steps.searchable_summary: Obtain mechanical strength test results for specimens at 7, 28, and 90 days curing time.
inputs: Specimens with different formulations (NDT/PC, CDT/PC ratios)
outputs: Mechanical strength values for each sample type at each curing time

steps.description: According to the way in which the samples are arranged in both graphs, it seems that the presence of diatomite in the formulations of the specimens, far from interfering negatively, rather causes a visible increase in mechanical resistance to compression from 7 to 90 days of curing.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze the trend of mechanical strength increase over time (7 to 90 days) for specimens containing diatomite.
inputs: Mechanical strength values over time for different formulations
outputs: Assessment of the effect of diatomite presence on mechanical strength increase over time

steps.description: Based on the above, not only is the pozzolanic character established once again in the samples studied, but also their ability to replace Portland cement and provide mechanical resistance to the specimens.
steps.searchable_summary: Interpret mechanical strength results to confirm pozzolanic character and assess the ability of diatomite formulations to replace Portland cement while providing mechanical resistance.
outputs: Confirmation of pozzolanic character based on mechanical strength
Assessment of Portland cement replacement potential based on mechanical resistance

steps.description: A detailed analysis of Figure 6a shows some remarkable evidence: (1) the greater the replacement of PC by diatomite (NDT/PC: 40%), the mechanical strength values increase very slowly, which is typical in pozzolanic reactions [41]; (2) when substitution decreases to 25% (NDT/PC: 25%), it seems that pozzolanic reactivity is reinforced, a fact that becomes more obvious after 28 days of curing; and (3) when the substitution is 10%...
steps.searchable_summary: Perform detailed analysis of mechanical strength trends for specific replacement ratios (e.g., 40%, 25%, 10%) over curing time to understand the relationship between substitution level, pozzolanic reaction kinetics, and strength development.
inputs: Mechanical strength values for specific NDT/PC replacement ratios at 7, 28, 90 days
outputs: Identification of specific trends in strength development related to replacement ratio and curing time
